steps.title: Compare the two positions
steps.content: Text 1 presents the publicity campaign as correcting a history in which women writers had to use male pseudonyms to receive serious consideration. Text 2 pushes back by calling that belief an overgeneralization.

steps.title: Use the counterexamples
steps.content: Text 2 points to Elizabeth Barrett Browning, who published under her own name, and Sara Payson Willis, who chose a female pseudonym. Those examples challenge the broad assumption that women writers were generally barred from publishing under names perceived as female.

steps.title: Reject agreement or new claims
steps.content: Choice B incorrectly makes Text 2 agree with Text 1. Choice C invents a split claim about which writers had the same motivations. Choice D focuses on the number of works published, but Text 2’s objection is about the pseudonym assumption, not productivity.

steps.title: Clarify the disagreement
steps.content: Text 2 does not deny that some women used male pseudonyms or that the campaign restored names for the listed works. Its objection is narrower: the historical claim behind Text 1 is too broad because some women published under female-perceived names or chose female pseudonyms.

steps.title: State the likely response
steps.content: The author of Text 2 would likely say that Barrett Browning and Willis are historical examples that complicate Text 1’s characterization of the campaign and its underlying assumption about women’s pseudonyms. The answer is A.
